article.7 Robustness: TheInternet Appendixillustrates additional robustness tests. First, the sensitivity of the results to additional definitions of investors and noninvestors are explored in Tables IA.15 through IA.17. Table IA.15 uses a fully-interacted model with income sorted into terciles, quartiles, and quintiles; Table IA.16 defines investor status using median income from the 1990 decennial census; and Table IA.17 explores the intersection of median income from the 1990 decennial census and the 2012 ACS. In all cases, the results are similar, although not quite significant (p= .13) for the relationship between market returns and violent crime in low-income locations when using 1990 census income alone. A potential explanation for the somewhat weaker results using the 1990 census data could be that later incomes may be a better indicator of the trend of income (and shareholder status) for those communities (e.g., through the slow process of gentrification). Second, the robustness of the crime definitions are examined using alternate categories of crimes as defined by NIBRS and listed inTable 1: crimes against persons, crimes against property, and crimes against society; additionally an alternative definition of violent crime from the FBI’s Summary Reporting System (SRS) is examined, and assaults alone.26Table IA.18 shows that the pattern for crimes against persons, SRS violent crimes, and assaults alone is similar to that for violent crimes as defined in this paper (assaults and homicides), while the pattern for crimes against property and society are similar to the overall crime rate. Third, the role of law enforcement officers is investigated in Table IA.19. The results do not appear to be driven by officers varying their enforcement across any of the category crimes, but rather driven by the occurrence of the incidents themselves. Fourth, alternate fixed effect specifications are considered in Table IA.20. The results are not sensitive to more restrictive or lenient forms of fixed effects. Fifth, a positive return indicator is examined to see whether outlier returns are influencing the relationship, and positive and negative indicators are interacted with the market return to examine whether the relationship differs in the positive and negative return domain. Panel A in Table IA.21 shows that outlier returns are not driving the relationship. Panel B provides suggestive evidence that investors are more sensitive to negative returns while noninvestors are more sensitive to positive returns, but the coefficients for the interacted positive and negative return indicators are not significantly different, which also provides evidence against a kink at the origin as suggested by prospect theory (Barberis, Huang, and Santos 2001). Sixth, extreme returns are further examined in Table IA.22 by adding indicators for market returns sorted into terciles, quartiles, and quintiles. The results are consistent with a negative relationship between violent crime and stock returns in high-income locations, and a positive relationship in low-income locations. Seventh, various transformations of the violent crime rate are considered to determine if outliers are influencing the relationship: logs, winsorization, and censored z-scores. Table IA.23 suggests that the main results are robust to extreme violent crime rates. Eighth, the panel data have been collapsed into a single time series regression in Table IA.24, which generates similar results. Ninth, the influence of salient economic and noneconomic news is examined. Table IA.25 utilizes an indicator for major economic news release days. Panel A shows that economic news release days exhibit more violent crime across income levels, but the relationship between market returns and violent crimes remains similar. Panel B interacts the economic news release day indicator with market returns and provides suggestive evidence that investors are more sensitive to returns on noneconomic news release days, while noninvestors are more sensitive to returns on economic news release days, but the coefficients for the interacted economic and noneconomic news release days are not significantly different. These results are consistent with Table IA.21 that examines positive and negative returns, because economic news release days tend to have higher returns (Ai and Bansal 2018). Table IA.26 utilizes an indicator for salient noneconomic news days using a news pressure index constructed similar toEisensee and Strömberg (2007)that measures the number of minutes major news broadcasters dedicate to the top-three noneconomic news stories. Panel A shows that high noneconomic news pressure days do not exhibit a significant relationship with violent crime. Panel B interacts the high noneconomic news pressure indicator with market returns and shows that both investors and noninvestors exhibit a significant relationship with returns only on low noneconomic news pressure days, but the coefficients for the interacted high news pressure and low news pressure days are not significantly different. The significant results only on noneconomic news days suggests that individuals may pay more attention to the market when there is no other distracting news. Tenth, the leading and lagging relationship is estimated jointly with the current return. Table IA.27 shows that the contemporaneous relationship between violent crime and market returns is robust, but it also provides weak evidence of a relationship between the first lag of returns and violent crime in high-income locations.Engelberg and Parsons (2016)also find evidence of a relationship between lagged returns and hospitalizations. Lastly, a difference estimate with date or region-date fixed effects is used to ask a slightly different question: is the relationship between stock market returns and crime significantlydifferentbetween high- and low-income locations? Table IA.28 shows that the difference estimates are similar to the difference in the coefficients from the main results. This suggests that daily marketwide and regional omitted variables do not likely influence the relationship between stock market returns and crime.
article.8 Economic Model of Crime:
The results in this paper indicate a contrasting relationship between stock returns and violent crime for investors and noninvestors. One explanation that is consistent with this paper’s empirical findings are rational asset pricing models with utility over relative wealth. First, the literature that links utility and violent crime is discussed. Next, relative status forms of utility are proposed to explain the documented relationship.

8.1 Crime as marginal utility

The economics and criminology literature suggest that crime is a plausible measure of marginal utility because crime increases in bad economic states. Crime decreases with state GDP growth (Arvanites and Defina 2006), decreases with the regional components of the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index (Rosenfeld and Fornango 2007;Rosenfeld 2009), increases with unemployment (Freeman 1999), decreases with low-skilled legal wages (Gould, Weinberg, and Mustard 2002), and increases with income inequality (Fajnzylber, Lederman, and Loayza 2002). So, a reasonable assumption may be that crime can serve as a proxy for the marginal utility of high-frequency stock market returns.

The psychology literature also suggests that depressed individuals have an increased propensity for violent behavior (Oakley, Hynes, and Clark 2009;Fazel et al. 2015). Depression, anxiety, and chronic stress have been linked to low serotonin levels, while low serotonin levels also have been linked to increased violent behavior in mice, primates, and humans (Krakowski 2003;Seo, Patrick, and Kennealy 2008). Depression often has been used as a proxy for utility in the economics literature (Luttmer 2005), while neurotransmitters, such as dopamine and serotonin, have been linked to utility in the neuroeconomics literature (Bossaerts 2009). This suggests a connection between utility and violent crime.

8.2 Crime as marginal utility over relative wealth

Theoretical models of crime typically fall into two categories. Under the standard economic model of crime, criminal activity is optimally chosen based on the expected utility of that act (Becker 1968). The utility gained could be from the monetary value of stolen goods or from the morbid pleasure of committing a violent act upon a victim. Alternatively, a crime may be committed due to a visceral factor triggering a loss of control (Loewenstein 2000). Examples of loss of control include inducing a fight due to anger, or stealing a good because the offender believes they deserve it. Consistent with either model, it is posited that an increase in marginal utility (loss in utility) either leads an individual to commit a crime to restore utility back to its original level or causes an individual to lose control and commit a crime.27

A class of utility models that are consistent with the empirical finding of a negative relationship between market returns and violent crime for investors (high-income areas), and a positive relationship for noninvestors (low-income areas), are models with utility over relative wealth. In relative status or external habit models, such asAbel (1990)andGali (1994), habit consumption is determined by everyone else’s consumption. For example, utility is represented asu(c/c¯)⁠, wherecis an individual’s consumption,c¯is external habit consumption (e.g., the average consumption of others), and consumption is subject to a budget constraint on wealth and income. If utility depends on relative consumption, then one person’s increase in consumption has a negative externality on others because it lowers the relative consumption of others. Since expected lifetime consumption is a function of wealth, relative wealth should also drive utilityu(w/w¯)and have similar effects. Specifically, similar toCard and Dahl (2011), it is assumed that with some probabilityp≥0⁠, the propensity for crime depends on some linear function of marginal utility over relative wealth:

λ p = p 0 + λ u ′ ( w / w ¯ ) , (3)

wherep0is the baseline probability of crime, andλis a positive constant. When marginal utility increases due to a decline in relative wealth, the probability of committing a crime increases.

Under utility over relative wealth, stock market increases do not necessarily correspond to declines in marginal utility (gains in utility). A market increase can have different impacts on investors and noninvestors. Although a market increase will lead to an increase in relative wealth and thus utility for investors, market increases cause noninvestors to be worse off. This is because noninvestors’relativewealthdecreasesas compared to the increase in average absolute wealth in the economy. This can be seen inFigure 4, where the propensity for crime (equivalently, marginal utility) is decreasing and convex in returns for investors, while increasing and convex for noninvestors.28

Fig. 4 Stockholder and nonstockholder crime propensity This figure illustrates the hypothetical crime propensity (marginal utility) for stockholders and nonstockholders for a given market return. Open in new tab Download slide

Furthermore, noninvestors still may be worse off despite the economic improvements that positive market returns may signal. For example,Piketty and Saez (2003)andSaez and Zucman (2016)show that gains in income and wealth have disproportionately accrued to the top of the distribution during economic expansions. This suggests that although on an absolute basis, noninvestors (low-income individuals) may be better off with economic improvements, on a relative basis they may fall behind. This is also consistent with the animosity over inequality that has grown over recent years.

Although the empirical results are consistent with utility over relative wealth, there are a few caveats. First, testing relative wealth models are problematic because changes in absolute wealth are difficult to distinguish from changes in relative wealth, because they are often correlated. Additionally, in models such asAbel (1990)andGali (1994), the importance of relative wealth tends to decline with absolute wealth.29While the data does not necessarily allow us to differentiate between effects from absolute and relative wealth for investors, this is not the case for noninvestors who have no wealth in the stock market. Second, relative status models require a reference group to which the individual compares themselves, which is often unobservable and has little consensus. For example,Veblen (1899)develops one of the earliest relative status frameworks where the reference group is defined as the wealthyleisure classthat the rest of society attempts to emulate throughconspicuous consumption. Alternatively, the reference group is often defined as those that live in the same area, such as the same county or state (Luttmer 2005;Daly, Wilson, and Johnson 2013).

While the exact reference group for individuals is unobservable, it is reasonable to assume that any relative status effects should be stronger in those communities that are more social (Luttmer 2005). Social interactions can amplify relative status effects through either learning facts or other’s beliefs regarding the stock market, or direct social comparisons (Hong, Kubik, and Stein 2004;Bursztyn et al. 2014). The average of two proxies for socialness is examined: church and bar attendance. Table IA.29 interacts market returns with indicators for above- and below-median social locations and shows that the magnitude of the relationship between the stock market and violent crime is stronger and significant only for those locations that are more social. Additionally, the difference in the interacted coefficients between social and nonsocial locations is significant for low-income locations (p= .04), but not quite significant for high-income locations (p= .13). In addition to the socialness proxy, we can alternatively examine whether the relative status effects are stronger in those areas where status comparisons are more likely with known “peers.” Peer comparisons are proxied for byeconomic connectedness(exposure) using Facebook connections between (memberships for) high- and low-socioeconomic status (SES) individuals fromChetty et al. (2022). Table IA.30 (Table IA.31) interacts market returns with indicators for counties with above- and below-median economic connectedness (exposure) and shows that the magnitude is larger and generally significant only for those locations that have more connections (exposure) between high- and low-SES individuals. The stronger relationship in more social and economically connected (exposed) locations provides additional suggestive evidence that concerns over relative status are driving the results. TheInternet Appendixalso explores an alternative measure that can drive changes in relative status as a replacement for market returns: mimicking portfolios for the growth in income and wealth share of the top 1%. Table IA.32 indicates that the alternative measures are consistent with the results using market returns, which also supports the relative status narrative. Further discussion on the socialness and peer comparison proxies, and alternative relative status drivers are provided in theInternet Appendix.
article.9 Conclusion:
This paper proposes crime as a measure of psychological well-being and provides evidence that both investor and noninvestor well-being is affected by changes in the stock market. A contemporaneous negative (positive) relationship between daily stock market returns and violent crime is found for investors (noninvestors). A similar relationship is found between local earnings surprises and local violent crime controlling for common daily news with date fixed effects.

The relationship between market returns and violent crime rates is robust to a battery of controls, and does not appear to be driven by alcohol consumption, or an increase in targets of violence outside the home. Focusing on the circumstances of violent crime for investors, the relationship is particularly strong for at-home violence by men against their wives and girlfriends, suggesting that concerns over personal finances and future consumption may be a source of conflict within the household.

One potential explanation that is consistent with the empirical finding of a contrasting relationship between stock returns and violent crime for investors and noninvestors is that changes in relative wealth may be a determinant of changes in psychological well-being. For investors, positive market returns increase their well-being because their wealth increases relative to an individual with average wealth. This increase in well-being corresponds to a decrease in violent crime for investors. For noninvestors, positive market returns decrease their well-being because they have no stock holdings and are now worse off relative to an individual with average wealth. This decrease in well-being corresponds to an increase in crime for noninvestors. In support of relative status influencing well-being, the contrasting relationships are strongest in locations that are more social and that have more “peers” of the opposite socioeconomic status. Combined, the results suggest that high income inequality and large stock market changes can be detrimental to society through increased crime. In addition, the results suggest that individuals may be better off if they actively ignore the stock market’s daily performance, as many financial advisors recommend.

If measures of psychological well-being such as violent crime or hospitalizations (Engelberg and Parsons 2016) proxy for marginal utility, it would be interesting to understand how these measures could be used in asset pricing. Specifically, these proxies for marginal utility could be used to estimate the stochastic discount factor and price the cross-section of stock returns. This is left for future work.
